Facts.text: Epoch (origin) of the Kali Yuga
Kʼakʼ Yipyaj Chan Kʼawiil, known as Smoke Shell, ascends to the throne of the Maya city of Copán
Jerusalem is retaken by the Christian Crusader kingdom in a peace treaty between Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II and Egyptian ruler Al-Kamil
Battle of Wesenberg: Livonian Brothers of the Sword fight the forces of Novgorod and Pskov, led by Dmitry of Pereslavl near Rakvere in modern-day Estonia; both sides claim victory
Emperor of Ethiopia Amda Seyon I begins his campaigns in the southern Muslim provinces
Treaty of Zadar: The Venetian Republic loses influence over Dalmatia in exchange for ending hostilities with the King of Hungary and Croatia, Louis I
Huguenot Jean de Poltrot assassinates French General Duke François de Guise with a shot from his pistol
Zeeland falls to Dutch rebels
Fort St. Louis is established by a Frenchman at Matagorda Bay, forming the basis for France's claim to Texas
Quakers consider drafting formal protest of slavery in Germantown, Pennsylvania
French invade under Jacques Cassard on Curacao
First opera performed in America, "Flora," in Charleston, South Carolina
Holy Roman Emperor Joseph II bans children under 8 from labor
Trinidad is surrendered to a British fleet under the command of Sir Ralph Abercromby
Ohio legislature approves establishment of Ohio University at Athens as the 1st university in Ohio; first classes are held in 1809
Ohio University in Athens, Ohio, becomes the first U.S. land-grant college when it is chartered
More than 100 vessels destroyed in a storm, Gibraltar
US labor newspaper, "The Man" begins publishing as a daily, in NYC
Detroit Boat Club forms and still exists
First regular steamboat service to California starts (or Feb 28)
California Legislature creates nine Bay Area counties
Battle of Fort Moultrie, SC occupied by Federals
Evacuation of Charleston, South Carolina
Union troops force Confederates to abandon Ft Anderson, NC
Direct telegraph link established between Britain and New Zealand
John Tunstall is murdered by outlaw Jessie Evans, sparking the Lincoln County War in Lincoln County, New Mexico.
Arabs capture Egyptian premier Nabar Pasha
British General Charles Gordon arrives in Khartoum
Capt Archinard's army fights with Nyamina of Niger in West-Sudan
Cave of Winds at Niagara Falls goes almost dry for first time in 50 yrs
80°F recorded in San Francisco, California
San Francisco named as a port of dispatch for Army transports
Stanley Cup, Montreal Arena, Westmount, Quebec: Montreal Victorias beat Winnipeg Victorias, 3-2 to sweep series, 2-0 for CAHL
Battle at Paardeberg, 1,270 British killed/injured
British troops occupy Monte Christo, Natal
H Cecil Booth patented a dust removing suction cleaner
1st US postage stamps in rolls issued
The American ambassador to Japan is given a note by the Japanese in which they agree to restrict Japanese emigration to the US; this becomes known as the 'Gentlemen's Agreement'
The first official flight with air mail takes place in Allahabad, British India, when Henri Pequet, a 23-year-old pilot, delivers 6,500 letters to Naini, about 10 km away.
President Francisco Madero of Mexico is overthrown
Germany begins a blockade of Britain
The last German garrison in the German colony of Cameroons surrenders
First major strike of the Russian "February Revolution" starts at the giant Putilov factory in Petrograd [NS=Mar 3]
Germany renews its offensive against the Russians, making dramatic gains against disorganized and dispirited Russian troops
British troops occupy Dublin
The Capper-Volstead Act allows farmers to buy and sell cooperatively without the risk of prosecution under antitrust laws
WOC-AM in Davenport IA begins radio transmissions
Belgium: Borinage-mine workers strike for higher wages
1st US radio broadcast of "Cities Service Concerts"
US & Canada open diplomatic relations
Johan Grøttumsbråten of Norway wins the Nordic combined gold at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; doubles up with the 18k cross country gold the previous day
Norway goes 1-2 in the ski jumping at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics with Alf Andersen taking gold ahead of Sigmund Ruud
USA 2 beats USA 1 by 0.5s for the 5-man bobsleigh gold medal at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; Germany 2 takes bronze
American astronomer Clyde Tombaugh discovers the dwarf planet Pluto
Cow flown & milked, milk sealed in paper containers & parachuted
Luigi Pirandello's "Come Tu Mi Vuoi" premieres in Milan
Japan proclaims and names Manchukuo in Manchuria, China, as an independent state
Norway'sSonja Heniewins her 6th straight World Women's figure skating title
NHL record 32 points scored, NY Americans (28) & Mont Maroons (24)
Golden Gate International Exposition opens on Treasure Island
Japanese troop land on Bali
1st edition of Dutch resistance newspaper "Trouw"
A syndicate headed by New York lumberman William D Cox buys MLB's Philadelphia Phillies for $850,000; 33 year-old Cox is youngest owner in baseball
German Propaganda Minister Josef Goebbells demands "total war" from German citizens in speech at Berlin Sportpalast
Members of student non-violent resistance group "White Rose" arrested by Nazis after distributing pamphlets in Munich, Germany
15-year-old Joe Nuxhall signs a contract to play baseball with the Cincinnati Reds just one day after playing in a high school basketball game; debuts later that year
Maastricht resistance fighter JAJ Janssen arrested
24 die in a train crash in Gallitzin, Pennsylvania
Gian Carlo Menotti's opera "Telephone" premieres in NYC
3 City College of NY basketball players admit to accepting bribes
Nepal becomes a constitutional monarchy
Netherlands Radio School forms
Norwegian speed skater Hjalmar Anderson wins his 3rd gold medal of the Oslo Winter Olympics when he claims the 10,000m in Olympic record 16:45.8; also wins gold in 1,500m and 5,000m
KOLN TV channel 10 in Lincoln, NB (CBS) begins broadcasting
The first Church of Scientology is established in Los Angeles, California
Baghdad Pact signed, making Turkey & Iraq a defense alliance
Kenyan freedom fighter Dedan Kimathi executed by hanging by the British colonial government
VIII Winter Olympic Games open in Squaw Valley, California
Walter O'Malley, LA Dodger owner, purchases Chavez Ravine for $494,000
Henk van der Grift becomes world champion skater
4th Daytona 500: Race winner Fireball Roberts dominates the event, leading 144 of the 200 laps
France & Algerian Muslims negotiate truce to end 7 year war
Muriel Resnik's "Any Wednesday" premieres in NYC
Papandreou government takes power in Greece
27 copper miners die in avalanche, Granduc Mountain, British Columbia
Frank Gifford announces his retirement from football for broadcasting
The Gambia becomes independent from the United Kingdom
Softball pitcher Eddie Feigner strikes out 6 straight major leaguers
After winning men's luge singles Olympic gold medal in Innsbruck (1964), Thomas Köhler of Germany teams with Klaus Bonsack to take out the doubles in Grenoble
Britain commences a trial of year-round daylight saving time (BST - British Standard Time, one hour ahead of GMT)
Thousands of people in West Berlin demonstrate against US involvement in the Vietnam War
X Winter Olympic Games close in Grenoble, France
Doug Walters scores 2nd innings century after 242 in 1st
PLO-attack El-Al plane in Zurich Switzerland
Chicago 7 defendants found innocent of inciting to riot
California Supreme Court abolishes death penalty
Giulio Andreotti sworn in as premier of Italy for the 1st time
54-kg octopus measuring 7m across captured in Hood Canal, Washington
Belgian Emiel Puttemans runs 3,000m indoor record 7:39.2
NASA launches Italian satellite San Marcos C-2 (235/843 km)
US ambassador to India Daniel Moynihan present $2,046,700,000 check
Italy broadens abortion law
American rock band KISS play their first concert in their hometown venue of Madison Square Garden in New York City
Space Shuttle Enterprise takes its maiden flight affixed atop a Boeing 747 Shuttle Aircraft Carrier
West Indies cricket fast bowlers Joel Garner (4/130) and (Colin Croft 3/85) debut in drawn 1st Test v Pakistan at Bridgetown, Barbados
1st Iron Man Triathlon (swim, bike ride, and run marathon) held, beginning and ending at the Aloha Tower in Honolulu Harbor of Oʻahu, Hawaii
-52°F (-47°C), Old Forge, New York (state record)
Mini-series "Roots: Next Generations" premieres on ABC TV
NASA launches space vehicle S-202
Snow falls in the Sahara Desert
East German cross country skier Barbara Petzold wins first of 2 gold medals at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics in women's 10k; also takes gold as part of 4 x 5k relay team
Pierre Elliott Trudeau's Liberal Party wins Canadian elections
Soviet figure skater Irina Rodnina wins her 3rd consecutive Olympic pairs title at Lake Placid; gold medals with husband Alexander Zaitsev (1976, 80) and Alexei Ulanov (1972)
Indiana Pacers lose, 121-94 in Milwaukee; begin a 28 NBA game losing streak on the road
Right wing Lanny McDonald becomes the first player in NHL franchise Calgary Flames' history to score 50 goals in a season in a 5-1 loss to Buffalo Sabres
East German figure skater Katarina Witt wins first of 2 consecutive women's singles gold medals at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics; also wins gold in Calgary (1988)
Finnish cross-country skier Marja-Liisa Hämäläinen wins the 20k gold medal at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics; sweeps all 3 individual events, also winning 5k and 10k events
Revised Concordat between Italy and Vatican is signed
San Antonio guard Alvin Robertson scores NBA's 2nd quadruple double-20 points, 11 rebounds, 10 assists & 10 steals in 120-114 win over Phoenix Suns
Federal Road Safety Corps is established in Nigeria
American amateur astronomer Peter Collins discovers Nova Cygni 1992, observed from Boulder, Colorado
Shreveport Pirates join Canadian Football League as 4th US-based team; fold 1995
38th Daytona 500: Dale Jarrett wins his 2nd Great American race ahead ofDale Earnhardtand Ken Schrader
Two white separatists are arrested in Nevada and accused of plotting a biological attack on NYC subways
Stjepan Mesić becomes the second President of Croatia
43rd Daytona 500: Michael Waltrip wins tragic race; last lap crash claims life ofDale Earnhardt, prompting sport to implement new safety procedures
Comet C/2002 V1 (NEAT) makes perihelion, seen by SOHO.
Nearly 200 people die in the Daegu subway fire in South Korea
A runaway freight train carrying sulfur, petrol, and fertilizer catches fire and explodes near Neyshabur in Iran, killing 295 people, including 182 rescue workers
49th Daytona 500: Kevin Harvick wins by 0.02s over Mark Martin in the closest finish since the first race at Daytona in 1959

The Directorate General of Budget, Accounting and Statistics announced that the Taiwanese economy has contracted an unprecedented 8.36% in the fourth quarter of 2008; thus placing the country in a technical recession
$50 million worth of diamonds is stolen in an armed robbery at Brussels Airport, Belgium
15 people are killed by flooding and landslides in Indonesia
American snowboarder David Wise wins first of 2 consecutive Olympic halfpipe gold medals in Sochi; wins again in Pyeongchang (2018)
Netherlands sweeps the medals in the men's 1,000m speed skating at the Sochi Winter Olympics; Jorrit Bergsma wins gold ahead of teammates Sven Kramer and Bob de Jong
Police clash with protesters in Bangkok, Thailand
60th Daytona 500: Austin Dillon wins after multi-car wreck sends race into overtime (207 laps) ahead of rookie Darrell Wallace Jr
After taking out the men's combined gold medal, Austrian alpine skier Marcel Hirscher wins the giant slalom at the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics
Aseman Airlines flight crashes in the Zagros mountains, Iran, killing all 66 on board
Frenchman Martin Fourcade wins his 2nd of 3 gold medals at the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics in the 15k mass start biathlon; also wins men's 12.5k pursuit & mixed relay
Syrian government forces begin a new offensive on Eastern Ghouta in Syria's civil war
25-year old murder of a woman solved when police in Minnesota run DNA though a genealogy site and find suspect

Duke of Clarence George Plantagenet is convicted of treason against his older brotherEdward IVof England and is privately executed in the Tower of London, allegedly by being drowned in a butt of Malmsey wine
Henry Tudor (laterHenry VIII) is appointed Prince of Wales, heir apparent to the English throne
Hernán Cortésleaves Cuba for the Yucatán Peninsula, Mexico, with 11 ships and 500 men
KingFrancis I of Franceand Suleiman I of the Ottoman Empire sign a military and trade agreement against Holy Roman EmperorCharles V
Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIcharges commanderAlbrecht von Wallenstein's with high treason and orders him brought to Vienna, dead or alive
John Bunyan's Christian novel "The Pilgrim's Progress" is published in London by Nathaniel Ponder
Bonnie Prince Charlie's troops occupy Inverness, Scotland
The Battle of Montereau; victory of French underNapoleon Bonaparteagainst Austrians and Württembergers under the King of Württemberg.
The American Party (Know-Nothings) convenes in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania to nominate its first Presidential candidate, former PresidentMillard Fillmore.
Chinese residents in the fledging state of Sarawak rebel against the "White Rajah"James Brooke
Confederate PresidentJefferson Davisinaugurated at Montgomery, Alabama (US Civil War)
Russian police seize all copies ofLeo Tolstoy's book "What I Believe In"
Mark Twainpublishes "The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn" in the US
The Barnum Museum opens in Bridgeport, Connecticut, dedicated to the life ofP. T. Barnum
Winston Churchillmakes his maiden speech in the British House of Commons.
Jules Massenet's opera "Le jongleur de Notre-Dame" ( The Hunchback of Notre Dame) premieres in Monte Carlo
"In Dahomey" the first African American musical to perform on Broadway opens at the New York Theater, starring George Walker and Bert Williams with music by Will Marion Cook
Dutch government ofAbraham Kuyperlaunches anti-strike laws
Frank Wedekind's play "Hidalla oder Sein und Haben" (Hidalla, or Being and Having) premieres in Munich
Vincent d'Indy's "Jour D'été à La Montagne" premieres in Paris
Boston Red Sox tradeCy Young, at 41, to Cleveland Naps
French modernist painting "Nude Descending a Staircase" byMarcel Duchampcauses an uproar when shown in New York
Cy Dennenyof NHL Ottawa Senators scores record 52nd goal
Kenesaw Mountain Landisresigns his federal judgeship to give full attention to job as Major League Baseball Commissioner
US Secretary of the Navy Edwin Denby resigns due toTeapot Domescandal
Sonja Henieof Norway wins the women’s figure skating gold medal at the St. Moritz Winter Olympic Games; her first of 3 consecutive titles in the event
Richard RodgersandLorenz Hart's musical "Simple Simon" premieres in NYC
4th Emmy Awards: First time awards are presented on a nationwide basis; "The Red Skelton Show," Sid Caesar, andImogene Cocawin
Continental League PresidentBranch Rickeyand Toronto team owner Jack Kent Cooke announce the opening date of their new baseball league as April 18, 1961
Betty Comden&Adolph GreenandJule Styne's musical "Fade Out-Fade In", featuringCarol Burnett, Dick Shawn (replacing Jack Cassidy), and Tina Louise, returns from lay-off at Mark Hellinger Theater, NYC
Church deaconJimmie Lee Jacksonis beaten and shot during a peaceful march in Marion, Alabama. His death 8 days later inspires theSelmato Montgomery marches.
British guitaristDavid Gilmourjoins progressive rock groupPink Floyd
US PresidentRichard Nixonlaunches the "Nixon doctrine"
John LennonandYoko Onoend a week of co-hosting the syndicated "TheMike DouglasShow", in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
15th Daytona 500:Richard Pettywins his 4th Great American Race by more than 2 laps from Bobby Isaac
George Harrisonreleases music single "True Love", from his "33-1/3" album
21st Daytona 500:Richard Pettywins after race leadersCale Yarboroughand Donnie Allison collide with each other on final lap; first 500 mile race broadcast from start to finish
PresidentZiaur Rahman's National Party wins Bangladeshi general election
BassistBill Wymansays he will leave Rolling Stones in 1983 (Sure!)

Twenty-year-old Edmonton centerWayne Gretzkybecomes the first player in NHL history to score five career hat tricks before age 21, scoring five goals and two assists in a 9-2 Oilers home win against St. Louis
Finnish ski jumperMatti Nykänenwins first of 4 career Olympic gold medals in the Large Hill individual event at the Sarajevo Winter Games
Anti-smoking ad airs for 1st time on TV, featuringYul Brynner: he died of smoking-induced lung cancer on 10th October 1985
Anthony Kennedy, sworn in as 106th US Supreme Court Justice
"A Better Man" single released byClint Black(Billboard Song of the Year 1989)
32nd Daytona 500: Derrike Cope wins afterDale Earnhardtshreds right rear tyre on last lap
Edmonton Oiler goalieGrant Fuhrreturns to NHL after season-long suspension for substance abuse & shuts out NJ Devils 4-0
Dan Jansenskates world record 1000m (1:12.43)
Andrea Bocellimakes his operatic debut as Rodolfo in "La bohème" at the Teatro Comunale in Cagliari
FBI agentRobert Hanssenis arrested for spying for the Soviet Union. He is ultimately convicted and sentenced to life in prison, where he dies
Greg Madduxsigns with the Chicago Cubs, the team he began his career with
Laureus World Sports Awards, Marinsky Theatre, St. Petersburg, Russia: Sportsman:Roger Federer; Sportswoman:Justine Henin; Team: South African Men's National Rugby Union team
English fantasy authorTerry Pratchettreceives a knighthood fromQueen Elizabeth IIat Buckingham Palace
Bibliothèque nationale de France purchases the memoirs ofGiacomo Casanovaa for €7 million
Pope Francisquestions US Presidential hopefulDonald Trump's Christianity over his call to build a wall on the Mexican border
16 US states, including California and New York, band together to sue PresidentDonald Trumpover his use of emergency powers to build a border wall.
Australian political parties hacked by a "sophisticated state actor" Prime MinisterScott Morrisonsays, just months ahead of general election
French SculptorFrédéric-Auguste Bartholdiis awarded a US patent for his design for the Statue of Liberty

Ukrainian Revolution of 2014 begins as protesters, riot police and unknown shooters take part in violent events in the capital, Kiev, culminating after five days in the ouster of PresidentViktor Yanukovych
Nasir al-Din al-Tusi, Persian philosopher, architect and mathematician (Tadhkirah fi ʿilm al-hay), born in Ṭūs, Khorāsān (d. 1274)
Jadwiga of Poland, Saint and Queen of Poland whose marriage with Jogaila, formed the Lithuanian-Polish Union, restored Kraków university, born in Breda, Hungary (d. 1399)
Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Bengali saint, inspired Chaitanya movement, born in Navadwip, India (d. 1534)
Uesugi Kenshin, Japanese samurai and warlord known as the "Dragon of Echigo", born in Echigo Province, Japan (d. 1578)
Charles III, Duke and ruler of Lorraine (1545-1608), know as "the Great", born in Nancy, Duchy of Lorraine  (d. 1608)
Isaac Casaubon, naturalized English classical scholar and theologian (Athenaeus), born in Geneva (d. 1614)
Per Brahe the Younger, Swedish soldier and statesman, Lord High Steward of Sweden, born in Rydboholm Castle, Sweden (d. 1680)
Edward Hyde, Earl of Clarendon, English PM (1660-67), born in Dinton, England (d. 1674)
Giovanni Battista Vitali, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1692)
Johan Göransson Gyllenstierna, Swedish statesman, born in Brännkyrka, Sweden (d. 1680)
Marie Champmeslé, French actress (Fête de Vénus), born in Rouen, France (d. 1698)
Charles-Irénée Castel de Saint-Pierre, French writer and reformist, one of the first to propose an international organization to maintain peace, born in Saint-Pierre-Église, France(d. 1743)
Jacques Cassini, French astronomer (proper motion. satellites of Saturn), born in Paris (d. 1756)
Gaspard Fritz, Swiss composer and violinist, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1783)
Karl Haack, German composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1819)
Johann Christian Heinrich Rinck, German composer, born in Elgersburg, Principality of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1846)
Friedrich Christian Ruppe, German-Dutch composer and violinist, brother of Christian Friedrich Ruppe, born in Salzungen, Imperial County of Reuss, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1834)
Giovanni Battista Brocchi, Italian naturalist and geologist (Conchiologia fossile subappennina), born in Bassano del Grappa, Republic of Venice (d. 1826)
Thomas Girtin, English artist, watercolorist and etcher ("The White House, Chelsea"), born in London (d. 1802)
John Parry [Bardd Alaw], Welsh composer and harpist, born in Denbigh, Wales (d. 1851)
Henry Martyn, English missionary (India and Persia), born in Truro, England (d. 1812)
Marshall Hall, English physician, physiologist and early neurologist (physiology of reflex function), born in Basford, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1857)
George Peabody, American merchant and philanthropist (Peabody Trust and Institute), born in South Danvers, Massachusetts (d. 1869)
Louis Malesherbes Goldsborough, American Rear Admiral (Union Navy during US Civil War), born in Washington D.C. (d. 1877)
Samuel Fenton Cary, American politician and temperance movement leader (Rep-IR-Ohio), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1900)
Jan August Hendrik Leys, Belgian Romantic painter, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1869)
Thomas D. Doubleday, American bookstore owner and Union Army officer, born in Albany, New York (d. 1864)
Lewis Armistead, American Confederate general, part of Pickett’s Charge during the Battle of Gettysburg, born in New Bern, North Carolina (d. 1863)
Walter Page Lane, American soldier and Confederate general who fought for Texas, born in County Cork, Ireland (d. 1892)
Joseph Philbrick Webster, American composer and songwriter ("Lorena", "In the Sweet By and BY"), born in Manchester, New Hampshire (d. 1875)
Carl Faust, Prussian military musician and composer (Marsch Glück auf - Lucky March), and conductor, born in Neisse, Province of Silesia, Prussia (d. 1892)
Jean Jacques Alfred Mouton, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army), born in Opelousas, Louisiana (d. 1864)
Rebecca Lee Crumpler, American physician and medical writer (first African American woman to receive a medical degree), born in Delaware (d. 1895)[1]
James Deshler, American Confederate general, born in Tuscumbia, Alabama (d. 1863)
Ramakrishna [Gadadhar Chatterji], Indian mystic and yogi (preached unity of religions), born in Kamarpukur, British India (d. 1886)
Ernst Mach, Austrian physicist (shock waves, Mach number), philosopher and psychologist, born in Brno, Moravia, Austrian Empire (d. 1916)
Samuel Prowse Warren, Canadian organist and composer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1915)
Willem Maris, Dutch painter (Hague School), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1910)
Wilson Barrett, British manager, actor, and playwright (The Sign of the Cross), born in Essex, England (d. 1904)
Louis Comfort Tiffany, American artist and designer known for his glasswork (Tiffany & Co), born in New York City (d. 1933)
Alexander Lange Kielland, Norwegian writer (Gist; Fortuna; St. Hans Fest), and politician (mayor; governor), born in Stavanger, Norway (d. 1906)
George Henschel, German-British baritone, pianist, conductor, and composer, born in Breslau, Silesia, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1934)
August Belmont Jr., American financier (construction of New York subway, NY Jockey Club), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1924)
Max Klinger, German graphic artist, painter and sculptor (Paraphrase on the Finding of a Glove), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1920)
Louise, princess of Belgium, married and divorced Philipp, Prince of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1924)
Anders Zorn, Swedish painter, etcher and sculptor (Gustavus Vasa), born in Mora, Sweden-Norway (d. 1920)
Boris Borisovich Golitsyn, prominent Russian physicist and inventor of the first electromagnetic seismograph, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1916)
Hedwig Courths-Mahler, German Romantic novelist whose novels sold 80 million copies (Warbride), born in Nebra (d. 1950)
William Laurel Harris, American mural painter (Church of Saint Paul the Apostle in NY), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1924)
Harry Brearley, English inventor of stainless steel, born in Sheffield, England (d. 1948)
Walter Andrae, German archaeologist (Babylon, Assur), born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1956)
Betsy Ranucci-Beckmann [Theodora Elisabeth Beckmann], Dutch author and actress (De huzaren, Dood Water), born in Bergen op Zoom, Noord-Brabant, Netherlands (d. 1965)
Eric De Lamarter, American organist, composer and conductor (Chicago Symphony), born in Lansing, Michigan (d. 1953)
Níkos Kazantzákis, prominent Greek writer and philosopher (Zorba the Greek, The Last Temptation of Christ), born in Kandiye, Ottoman Empire (d. 1957)
Tom Walls, British actor and producer (A Cuckoo in the Nest, Undercover), born in Kingsthorpe, England (d. 1949)
Andrew Watson Myles, Canadian politician, born in Rathwell, Manitoba (d. 1970)
Burton "Burt" Mustin, American actor (All in the Family, The Andy Griffith Show), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1977)
Cecil Parkin, English cricket spin bowler (10 Tests, 32 wickets, BB 5/38; Durham CCC) and author (Cricket Reminiscences: Humorous & Otherwise), born in Eaglescliffe, England (d. 1943)
George Mogridge, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1924 Washington Senators; no-hitter 1917 NY Yankees), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1962)
Sybold van Ravesteyn, Dutch architect (Blijdorp, CS Rotterdam, Utrecht), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1983)
Adolphe Menjou, American actor (Front Page; Star is Born), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1963)
Edward Arnold, American actor (Mr Smith Goes to Washington), born in New York City (d. 1956)
Lauri Haarla, Finnish writer (Juudas, Sukeltaja), born in Korpilahti, Finland (d. 1944)
Chester Clute, American actor ("Arsenic and Old Lace"; "Too Many Girls"; "Niagara Falls"), born in Orange, New Jersey (d. 1956)
Wendell Wilkie, American Republican presidential candidate (1940), and author (One World), born in Elwood, Indiana (d. 1944)
George "The Gipper" Gipp, American football player (Notre Dame), born in Laurium, Michigan (d. 1920)
Semyon Timoshenko, Russian military officer (Marshal of the Soviet Union - Soviet-Finnish War,  WWII Eastern Front), born in Orman, Bessarabia Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1970)
Charles Kuentz, Alsatian WW1 veteran, changed nationality 4 times, born in Ranspach, Alsace (d. 2005)
K'Tut Tantri [Muriel Stuart Walker] Scottish-American broadcaster during Indonesian National Revolution, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1997)
Luis Muñoz Marín, Puerto Rican poet, journalist, and politician (1st elected Governor of Puerto Rico, 1949-65), born in Old San Juan, Puerto Rico (d. 1980)
Walter Herbert [Seligmann], German-American conductor, impresario (Houston Grand Opera, 1955- 72; San Diego Opera, 1965-77), and world champion bridge player, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1975)
Arthur Bryant, English historian (King Charles II), born in Dersingham, England (d. 1985)
Reginald Sheffield, British actor (Suspicion, Of Human Bondage, Second Chance), born in London, England (d. 1957)
Nikolai Podgorny, Ukrainian Soviet statesman, Premier of the USSR (1965-77), born in Karlovka, Russian Empire (d. 1983)
Queenie Leonard, British actress, born in London, England (d. 2002)
Hans Asperger, Austrian pediatrician and eponym of Asperger syndrome, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1980)
Billy De Wolfe, American character actor (Good Morning World), born in Quincy, Massachusetts (d. 1974)
Angelo Rossitto, American dwarf actor (known as "Little Moe", Carousel), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1991)
Matti Järvinen, Finnish javelin thrower (Olympic gold 1932), born in Tampere, Finland (d. 1985)
Tuppy Owen-Smith, South African cricket batsman (5 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 129; Western Province) and rugby union fullback (10 Tests England), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1990)
Wallace Stegner, American novelist and critic (Pulitzer-1972 for Angle of Repose), born in Lake Mills, Iowa (d. 1992)
Dane Clark, American actor (Wire Service, Bold Venture, Perry Mason), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1998)
Hatsuo "Jerry" Fujikawa, American-Japanese actor (Matsu in "Mr. T and Tina", The Twilight Zone, The Man from U.N.C.L.E.), born in Monterey Country, California
Artur Axmann, German Nazi leader (head of Hitler Youth 1940-45), born in Hagen, Germany (d. 1996)
Pee Wee King [Julius Frank Kuczynski], American country singer, accordionist, and songwriter ("Tennessee Waltz"; "Slow Poke"), born in Abrams, Wisconsin (d. 2000)
Joe Gordon, American Baseball HOF 2nd baseman (World Series 1938, 39, 41, 43 NY Yankees; 1948 Cleveland Indians; AL MVP 1942; 9 x MLB All Star), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1978)
Marcel Landowski, French composer (Un enfant appelle), born in Pont-l'Abbé, Brittany (d. 1999)
Phyllis Calvert [Bickle], British actress (Crash of Silence, The Man in Grey), born in London, England (d. 2002)
Betty Gathergood, British curator (Dr. Johnson's House), born in the United States (d. 1996)[1]
Jean Drapeau, Canadian politician (Mayor of Montreal 1954-57, 1960-86), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1999)
Jane Loevinger, American psychologist who studied ego development, born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 2008)
Bill Cullen, American TV game show host (over 20 different games), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1990)
Jack Palance [Vladimir Palahniuk], American actor (City Slickers, Contempt, Batman), born in Lattimer Mines, Pennsylvania (d. 2006)
Eric Gairy, first Prime Minister of Grenada (1974-1979), born in Dumfermlaine, Grenada (d. 1997)
Helen Gurley Brown, American author/publisher and editor-in-chief of Cosmopolitan, born in Green Forest, Arkansas, (d. 2012)
Juhan Smuul, Estonian author, born in Muhu, Estonia (d. 1971)
Rolf "Hazy" Osterwald, Swiss jazz bandleader, trumpeter, and vibraphonist, born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 2012)
